question: Is the three-part pattern of the three angels' messages — (1) gospel call to worship the true God, (2) exposure of a false system, (3) warning of judgment — repeated throughout the Old Testament? Examine: Noah before the flood, Abraham and Sodom, Moses and Egypt, Elijah at Carmel, Josiah's reform, Jeremiah's ministry. Do these share the same structure as Rev 14:6-12?

topics.name: NOAH
topics.entry_text: 1. Son of Lamech GEN 5:28,29 -Builds an ark (ship) and saves his family from the great flood GEN 6:14-22; 7; 8; MAT 24:38; LUK 17:27; HEB 11:7; 1PE 3:20 -Builds an altar and offers sacrifices GEN 8:20,21 -Receives the covenant from God that no flood would ever again visit the earth; the rainbow instituted as a token of the covenant GEN 8:20,22; 9:9-17 -Intoxication of, and his curse upon Canaan GEN 9:20-27 -His blessing upon Shem and Japheth GEN 9:26,27 -Dies at the age of nine-hundred and fifty years GEN 9:28,29

topics.name: ELIJAH
topics.entry_text: 1. The Tishbite, a Gileadite and prophet. Persecuted by Ahab. Escapes to the wilderness, fed by ravens. Goes to Zarephath. Returns and sends message to Ahab. Meets Ahab and directs him to assemble the prophets of Baal 1KI 18:17-20. Derisively challenges the priests of Baal 1KI 18:25-29. Kills the prophets of Baal 1KI 18:40. Escapes to the wilderness from the fierceness of Jezebel. Fasts for forty days. His translation 2KI 2:11. Appears to Jesus at his transfiguration. Antitype of John the Baptist. MIRACLES OF: Causes fire to consume the sacrifice 1KI 18:24,36-38. Causes rain after seven years of drought 1KI 18:41-45; JAS 5:17,18.

topics.name: SODOM
topics.entry_text: Also called SODOMA. Situated on the plain of the Jordan River GEN 13:10. Lot lived in GEN 13:12. Wickedness of the inhabitants of GEN 13:13; 19:4-13; DEU 32:32; ISA 3:9; JER 23:14; EZK 16:46,48,49; Jude 1:7. Abraham's intercession for GEN 18:16-33. Destroyed on account of the wickedness of the people GEN 19:1-29; DEU 29:23; ISA 13:19; AMO 4:11; MAT 10:15; LUK 17:29; 2PE 2:6. FIGURATIVE of wickedness DEU 32:32; ISA 1:10; EZK 16:46-56.

topics.name: FLOOD
topics.entry_text: (The deluge) Foretold GEN 6:13,17. History of GEN 6; 7; 8. References to JOB 22:16; PSA 90:5; MAT 24:38; LUK 17:26,27; HEB 11:7; 1PE 3:20; 2PE 2:5. The promise that it should not recur GEN 8:20,21; ISA 54:9.

topics.name: BAAL
topics.entry_text: 1. An idol of the Phoenicians, god of the sun. The Israelites wickedly worshiped in the time of the judges JDG 2:10-23. Under Ahab 1KI 16:31-33; 18:18; 19:18. Jeremiah expostulates against the worship of JER 2:8,23; 7:9. Altars of, destroyed by Gideon JDG 6:25-32. Destroyed by Josiah 2KI 23:4,5. Prophets of, slain by Elijah 1KI 18:40. All worshipers of, destroyed by Jehu 2KI 10:18-25.

topics.name: BABYLON
topics.entry_text: 1. CITY OF: Built by Nimrod GEN 10:10. Capital of the kingdom of Babylon DAN 4:30. Prophecies concerning PSA 87:4; ISA 13; 14:4-26; JER 50; 51. FIGURATIVE REV 14:8; 16:19; 17; 18. 2. EMPIRE OF: Founded by Nimrod GEN 10:10. Prophetic denunciations against PSA 137:8,9; ISA 13; JER 50; 51.

topics.name: JEREMIAH
topics.entry_text: 5. The prophet JER 1:1. A priest JER 1:1. Call of JER 1:4-19. Foretells the desolation of Jerusalem JER 19. Pashur scourges and casts him into prison JER 20:1-3. Imprisoned by Zedekiah. Prophecies of, written by Baruch JER 36:1-7,32. Prophecies of, studied by Daniel DAN 9:2. Lamentations over Josiah 2CH 35:25.

topics.name: JOSIAH
topics.entry_text: 1. King of Judah 2KI 21:24-26; 22:1. Killed in battle with Pharaoh-necho 2KI 23:29,30. Lamentations for 2CH 35:25. Piety of: exemplified in his repairing of the temple 2KI 22:3-7; 2CH 34:1-4. Solicitude when the copy of the law was discovered and read to him 2KI 22:8-20; 2CH 34:14-33. In keeping a solemn Passover 2KI 23:21-23. Destroys the altar and high places of idolatry 2KI 23:3-20,24,25.

topics.name: WORSHIP
topics.entry_text: To be rendered only to God EXO 20:3; DEU 5:7; 6:13; MAT 4:10; LUK 4:8; ACT 10:26; 14:15; COL 2:18; REV 19:10; 22:8. Acceptable to God GEN 4:4; 8:21. Of the wicked rejected GEN 4:5,7. Of Abraham GEN 12:7,8; 13:4,18. Of Jacob GEN 35:2,3.

topics.name: PREACHING
topics.entry_text: (The act of exhorting, prophesying, reproving, teaching) Solomon called 'the preacher' (Koheleth) ECC 1:1,12. Noah called 'preacher,' 2PE 2:5. Repentance, the subject of John the Baptist's MAT 3:2; MRK 1:4,15. The Gospel of the Kingdom of God, the subject of Christ's MRK 1:14,15.

topics.name: PLAGUE
topics.entry_text: As a judgment on the Egyptians PSA 105; 135:8,9; ACT 7:36. The plague of Blood EXO 7:14-25; Frogs EXO 8:1-15; Lice EXO 8:16-19; Flies EXO 8:20; On cattle EXO 9:1-7; Boils EXO 9:8-12; Hail EXO 9:18-34; Locusts EXO 10:1-20; Darkness EXO 10:21-23; Death of the firstborn EXO 11:4-7; 12:17,29,30.

topics.name: COVENANT
topics.entry_text: OF GOD WITH MEN: Confirmed with an oath GEN 22:16; 26:3; PSA 89:35; HEB 6:13,17,18. Binding LEV 26; JER 11:2,3; GAL 3:15. Everlasting GEN 8:20-22; 9:1-17; PSA 105:8,10. God faithful to LEV 26:44,45; DEU 4:31; 7:8,9; PSA 105:8-11; MIC 7:20.

topics.name: PROPHECY
topics.entry_text: The Lord GOD will do nothing, but he revealeth his secret unto his servants the prophets AMO 3:7. God spoke through prophets NUM 12:6. Holy men of God spake as they were moved by the Holy Ghost 2PE 1:21.

topics.name: RIGHTEOUSNESS
topics.entry_text: Noah was a just man and perfect in his generations, and Noah walked with God GEN 6:9. Noah the eighth person, a preacher of righteousness 2PE 2:5. By faith Noah condemned the world and became heir of the righteousness which is by faith HEB 11:7.

topics.name: GOSPEL
topics.entry_text: Everlasting gospel REV 14:6. The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ MRK 1:1. The power of God unto salvation ROM 1:16.

topics.name: JUDGMENTS
topics.entry_text: DESIGN OF: That my name may be declared throughout all the earth EXO 9:16. Against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments EXO 12:12. Upon their gods also the LORD executed judgments NUM 33:4. The watchman who blows the trumpet and warns the people EZK 33:3-5. The Lord GOD will do nothing, but he revealeth his secret unto his servants the prophets AMO 3:7.

topics.name: CONDESCENSION OF GOD
topics.entry_text: Shall I hide from Abraham that thing which I do GEN 18:17. My spirit shall not always strive with man GEN 6:3. The Lord is not slack concerning his promise, but is longsuffering to us-ward, not willing that any should perish 2PE 3:9.

verses.heading: First angel's message — everlasting gospel, fear God, worship the Creator
verses.kjv_text: And I saw another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach unto them that dwell on the earth, and to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people, Saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters.
verses.context: Revelation 14 follows the beast system of chapter 13. The three angels' messages (14:6-12) are the divine counter-proclamation. The first angel establishes the baseline pattern: gospel proclamation (euangelion aionion), call to worship (proskyneo), and announcement of judgment hour (krisis).
verses.notes: Three aorist imperatives: phobethete (fear), dote (give), proskynerate (worship). The Creation formula (heaven, earth, sea, fountains of waters) echoes Exodus 20:11. This verse establishes the three-part vocabulary chain to trace through OT episodes.

verses.heading: Second angel's message — Babylon is fallen, exposure of false system
verses.kjv_text: And there followed another angel, saying, Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city, because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication.
verses.context: The second angel exposes Babylon as a false system that has corrupted all nations. The reduplication 'Epesen epesen' (fell, fell) intensifies the declaration. The imagery of intoxicating wine draws directly from Jer 51:7.

greek_parsing.gloss: ἄλλος                ἄλλος           G243     [A-NSM] Nom Sg M
Pres Act Ptcp Nom Sg M
τοῦ                  ὁ               G3588    [T-GSM] Gen Sg M

verses.notes: Epesen epesen — two aorist indicative forms of pipto (G4098). The genitive chain tou oinou tou thymou tes porneias autes is complex: wine of the wrath of her fornication. Pepotiken (perfect active) — she has made to drink, indicating completed action with ongoing effects. Direct source text: Jer 51:7-8.

verses.heading: Third angel's message — judgment warning against false worship
verses.kjv_text: And the third angel followed them, saying with a loud voice, If any man worship the beast and his image, and receive his mark in his forehead, or in his hand, The same shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture into the cup of his indignation; and he shall be tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb: And the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever: and they have no rest day nor night, who worship the beast and his image, and whosoever receiveth the mark of his name. Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus.
verses.context: The third angel warns of judgment on those who choose false worship. The warning is urgent and specific. Verse 12 concludes with the character of those who heed the messages: commandment-keepers who hold the faith of Jesus.
verses.notes: Proskyneo appears twice in this passage — once for false worship (v.9) and once to describe those who persist in it (v.11). The thymos/orge wrath chain connects to the OT chemah/aph vocabulary via LXX. Fire and brimstone echo Gen 19:24 (Sodom destruction).

verses.heading: Come out of her, my people — the 'come out' tradition fulfilled
verses.kjv_text: And I heard another voice from heaven, saying, Come out of her, my people, that ye be not partakers of her sins, and that ye receive not of her plagues.
verses.context: Rev 18:4 is the NT culmination of the 'come out' tradition that traces from Jer 50:8, 51:6, 51:45 through Isa 48:20 and 52:11. The verb Exelthate (exerchomai, G1831, aorist active imperative) directly parallels the Hebrew yatsa (H3318) commands in Jeremiah and Isaiah.

greek_parsing.gloss: ἤκουσα               ἀκούω           G191     [V-AAI-1S] Aor Act Ind Sg firstPers
τοῦ                  ὁ               G3588    [T-GSM] Gen Sg M
Pres Act Ptcp Acc Sg F

verses.notes: Exelthate is aorist active imperative 2nd plural — a decisive command to come out. The purpose clause (hina me) gives the reason: to avoid sharing in sins and receiving plagues. This confirms the OT-to-NT vocabulary chain.

verses.heading: Song of Moses and the Lamb — Exodus-to-Revelation bridge
verses.kjv_text: And they sing the song of Moses the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb, saying, Great and marvellous are thy works, Lord God Almighty; just and true are thy ways, thou King of saints. Who shall not fear thee, O Lord, and glorify thy name? for thou only art holy: for all nations shall come and worship before thee; for thy judgments are made manifest.
verses.context: Rev 15:3-4 explicitly combines the Song of Moses (Exodus 15) with the Song of the Lamb, forming a typological bridge from the Exodus judgment-on-Egypt episode to the end-time scenario. The language echoes the first angel: fear, glorify, worship, judgments.
verses.notes: Key vocabulary overlap with Rev 14:7: phobeo (fear), doxa (glory/glorify), proskyneo (worship), krisis/krima (judgment). This confirms that Revelation itself treats the Exodus pattern as a type of the end-time three-part message.

verses.heading: Noah episode: 120-year probationary period — extended gospel opportunity
verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said, My spirit shall not always strive with man, for that he also is flesh: yet his days shall be an hundred and twenty years.
verses.context: God's statement limits human probation to 120 years before the flood. This represents extended gospel opportunity — time for Noah's preaching to take effect. The striving of God's Spirit is the gospel element.
verses.notes: The 120-year period shows the patience of God before judgment — the same principle seen in all six OT episodes and culminating in the 'everlasting gospel' of Rev 14:6.

verses.heading: Noah episode: exposure of world's moral corruption
verses.kjv_text: And GOD saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually. And it repented the LORD that he had made man on the earth, and it grieved him at his heart. And the LORD said, I will destroy man whom I have created from the face of the earth; both man, and beast, and the creeping thing, and the fowls of the air; for it repenteth me that I have made them.
verses.context: Genesis 6:5 exposes the world's condition: total moral corruption. Every imagination (yetser) of the thoughts (machsheboth) of the heart was only evil (ra) continually. This is the exposure element — the false system is universal wickedness.

hebrew_parsing.gloss: 'see') יְהוָ֔ה (YHWH) כִּ֥י (that) רַבָּ֛ה (רב adj.fs 'much') רָעַ֥ת (רעה noun.fs.cst 'evil') הָאָדָ֖ם (human) בָּאָ֑רֶץ (in the earth) וְכָל (and whole) יֵ֨צֶר֙ (יצר noun.ms.cst 'form/imagination') מַחְשְׁבֹ֣ת (מחשׁבה noun.fp.cst 'thought') לִבֹּ֔ו (לב noun.ms+3ms 'his heart') רַ֥ק (only) רַ֖ע (רע adj.ms 'evil') כָּל־הַיֹּֽום (all the day)

verses.notes: Hebrew parsing confirms ra'ath (evil, noun fs construct) and ra (evil, adj ms). The double use of 'kol' (whole/every) emphasizes the totality of corruption. This verse is the judgment basis — exposure comes before the judgment announcement in v.7.

verses.heading: Noah episode: earth corrupt (shachath) and filled with violence (chamas)
verses.kjv_text: The earth also was corrupt before God, and the earth was filled with violence. And God looked upon the earth, and, behold, it was corrupt; for all flesh had corrupted his way upon the earth.
verses.context: Genesis 6:11-12 uses shachath (H7843) in both Niphal ('the earth was corrupt') and Hiphil ('all flesh had corrupted his way'). The word chamas (H2555, violence) completes the exposure. These are the two key terms for the pre-flood condition.

hebrew_parsing.gloss: 'was corrupt/destroyed') הָאָ֖רֶץ (the earth) לִפְנֵ֣י (before) הָֽאֱלֹהִ֑ים (God) וַתִּמָּלֵ֥א (מלא Niphal.Wayq.3fs 'was filled') הָאָ֖רֶץ (the earth) חָמָֽס (חמס noun.ms 'violence')

verses.notes: Hebrew parser confirms: tishacheth (shachath, Niphal Wayyiqtol 3fs) — the earth became corrupt; chamas (noun ms absolute) — violence. The LXX translates shachath with diaphtheiro, establishing the Greek connection (TR-shachath-diaphtheiro).

verses.heading: Noah episode: God announces judgment and commands ark construction
verses.kjv_text: And God said unto Noah, The end of all flesh is come before me; for the earth is filled with violence through them; and, behold, I will destroy them with the earth. Make thee an ark of gopher wood; rooms shalt thou make in the ark, and shalt pitch it within and without with pitch.
verses.context: God reveals His judgment plan to Noah personally (Amos 3:7 principle) and commands the ark's construction. The word mashchitham (Hiphil participle of shachath + 3mp suffix) — 'I am about to destroy them' — uses the same root as the corruption in v.11-12.

hebrew_parsing.gloss: 'said') אֱלֹהִ֜ים (God) לְנֹ֗חַ (to Noah) קֵ֤ץ (end) כָּל־בָּשָׂר֙ (all flesh) בָּ֣א (בוא Qal.Perf.3ms 'has come') לְפָנַ֔י (before me) כִּֽי (because) מָלְאָ֥ה (מלא Qal.Perf.3fs 'is full') הָאָ֛רֶץ (the earth) חָמָ֖ס (violence) מִפְּנֵיהֶ֑ם (because of them) וְהִנְנִ֥י (behold I) מַשְׁחִיתָ֖ם (שׁחת Hiphil.Ptcp+3mp 'am destroying them') אֶת־הָאָֽרֶץ (with the earth)

verses.notes: Hebrew parser confirms mashchitam (shachath, Hiphil participle + 3mp suffix) — God uses the same verb for judgment that described their corruption. Wordplay: they corrupted (shachath) the earth, so God will destroy (shachath) them. The ark is the gospel provision — a way of escape during judgment.

verses.heading: Noah episode: flood judgment announced with covenant promise
verses.kjv_text: And, behold, I, even I, do bring a flood of waters upon the earth, to destroy all flesh, wherein is the breath of life, from under heaven; and every thing that is in the earth shall die. But with thee will I establish my covenant; and thou shalt come into the ark, thou, and thy sons, and thy wife, and thy sons' wives with thee.
verses.context: The judgment announcement (v.17) is immediately followed by the covenant promise (v.18). This structure mirrors the three-part pattern: judgment is announced, but salvation is offered to those who respond in faith.
verses.notes: The contrast between v.17 ('every thing that is in the earth shall die') and v.18 ('But with thee will I establish my covenant') perfectly illustrates the gospel/judgment duality in the three-part pattern.

verses.heading: Noah episode: final 7-day warning before flood judgment
verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Noah, Come thou and all thy house into the ark; for thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation. Of every clean beast thou shalt take to thee by sevens, the male and his female: and of beasts that are not clean by two, the male and his female. Of fowls also of the air by sevens, the male and the female; to keep seed alive upon the face of all the earth. For yet seven days, and I will cause it to rain upon the earth forty days and forty nights; and every living substance that I have made will I destroy from off the face of the earth.
verses.context: The final 7-day warning. Noah is called 'righteous' (tsaddiyq, H6662). The invitation 'Come thou and all thy house into the ark' is the last gospel call before judgment falls.
verses.notes: God's address to Noah as tsaddiyq (righteous) confirms the connection to 2 Pet 2:5 (keryx dikaiosynes — preacher of righteousness). The 7-day final warning parallels the pattern of last-chance warnings before judgment in all six episodes.

verses.heading: Noah as 'preacher of righteousness' — NT confirmation of gospel call
verses.kjv_text: And spared not the old world, but saved Noah the eighth person, a preacher of righteousness, bringing in the flood upon the world of the ungodly;
verses.context: Peter identifies Noah as keryx dikaiosynes — a herald (keryx, G2783) of righteousness (dikaiosyne, G1343). This confirms that Noah was not merely a boat-builder but a commissioned announcer of divine warning.

greek_parsing.gloss: ἀρχαίου              ἀρχαῖος         G744     [A-GSM] Gen Sg M
ἐφείσατο,            φείδομαι        G5339    [V-ADI-3S] Aor Mid Ind Sg thirdPers
ὄγδοον               ὄγδοος          G3590    [A-ASM] Acc Sg M
Aor Act Ptcp Nom Sg M

verses.notes: Greek parser confirms: keryxa (keryx, G2783, accusative singular masculine — herald/preacher); dikaiosynes (dikaiosyne, G1343, genitive singular feminine — of righteousness). Keryx is a formal herald commissioned by sovereign authority (see word study G2783). Only 3 NT occurrences.

verses.heading: Noah's faith condemned the world — exposure through righteous example
verses.kjv_text: By faith Noah, being warned of God of things not seen as yet, moved with fear, prepared an ark to the saving of his house; by the which he condemned the world, and became heir of the righteousness which is by faith.
verses.context: Hebrews explains how Noah's faith functioned as exposure: 'by the which he condemned the world.' His righteous response to God's warning implicitly exposed the world's wickedness.
verses.notes: The word 'condemned' (katekrinen) shows that Noah's faithful obedience served as judgment on the world — his righteousness exposed their wickedness. This is the exposure element operating through contrast rather than explicit accusation.

verses.heading: Abraham/Sodom: God reveals judgment plan — Amos 3:7 principle
verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said, Shall I hide from Abraham that thing which I do; Seeing that Abraham shall surely become a great and mighty nation, and all the nations of the earth shall be blessed in him? For I know him, that he will command his children and his household after him, and they shall keep the way of the LORD, to do justice and judgment; that the LORD may bring upon Abraham that which he hath spoken of him. And the LORD said, Because the cry of Sodom and Gomorrah is great, and because their sin is very grievous; I will go down now, and see whether they have done altogether according to the cry of it, which is come unto me; and if not, I will know.
verses.context: God reveals His plan to Abraham before acting — the Amos 3:7 principle in action. The exposure element: 'their sin is very grievous.' Abraham's subsequent intercession (18:23-33) demonstrates the gospel element — seeking mercy and appealing to divine justice.
verses.notes: God identifies the reason for judgment: the cry of Sodom is great, their sin is very grievous. Abraham appeals to God's character: 'Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?' (18:25). This intercession is the gospel element — seeking to save the righteous from the judgment.

verses.heading: Abraham/Sodom: angels enter as divine messengers — parallel to Rev 14 angels
verses.kjv_text: And there came two angels to Sodom at even; and Lot sat in the gate of Sodom: and Lot seeing them rose up to meet them; and he bowed himself with his face toward the ground; And he said, Behold now, my lords, turn in, I pray you, into your servant's house, and tarry all night, and wash your feet, and ye shall rise up early, and go on your ways. And they said, Nay; but we will abide in the street all night. And he pressed upon them greatly; and they turned in unto him, and entered into his house; and he made them a feast, and did bake unleavened bread, and they did eat.
verses.context: Two angels enter Sodom as divine messengers. This is the most direct structural parallel to Rev 14 — literal angels carrying God's message to a doomed city before judgment falls.
verses.notes: The Hebrew malak (H4397, angel/messenger) is the same word translated aggelos (G32) in the LXX and NT. The Sodom narrative features literal angel-messengers warning before judgment, just as Rev 14 features angel-messengers.

verses.heading: Abraham/Sodom: 'come out' call before judgment — parallel to Rev 18:4
verses.kjv_text: And the men said unto Lot, Hast thou here any besides? son in law, and thy sons, and thy daughters, and whatsoever thou hast in the city, bring them out of this place: For we will destroy this place, because the cry of them is waxen great before the face of the LORD; and the LORD hath sent us to destroy it. And Lot went out, and spake unto his sons in law, which married his daughters, and said, Up, get you out of this place; for the LORD will destroy this city. But he seemed as one that mocked unto his sons in law.
verses.context: The angels tell Lot to bring his family OUT of the city before destruction. This is the earliest 'come out' call in the Bible, directly paralleling Rev 18:4. The response pattern is also significant: Lot's sons-in-law mock the warning, just as scoffers reject the three angels' messages.
verses.notes: The verb 'bring them out' uses yatsa (H3318) in Hiphil — cause to go out. This is the same root verb that appears in Jer 51:45, Isa 48:20, and Isa 52:11 in the 'come out' tradition, and translates to exerchomai (G1831) in Rev 18:4.

verses.heading: Abraham/Sodom: fire judgment executed after warning
verses.kjv_text: Then the LORD rained upon Sodom and upon Gomorrah brimstone and fire from the LORD out of heaven; And he overthrew those cities, and all the plain, and all the inhabitants of the cities, and that which grew upon the ground.
verses.context: Judgment falls after warning has been given and the righteous have been extracted. The fire-and-brimstone language is echoed in Rev 14:10 (third angel's warning) and directly cited by Jesus in Luke 17:29.
verses.notes: Cross-testament parallels confirm: Luke 17:29 directly cites this verse; 2 Pet 2:6 and Jude 1:7 use Sodom as a paradigm for judgment. Rev 14:10 uses the same fire-and-brimstone imagery for the third angel's judgment warning.

verses.heading: Structural principle: God reveals plans to prophets before acting
verses.kjv_text: Surely the Lord GOD will do nothing, but he revealeth his secret unto his servants the prophets.
verses.context: This verse articulates the universal principle underlying all six OT episodes: God always warns before judging. Every episode features a prophetic figure (Noah, Abraham/angels, Moses, Elijah, Huldah/Josiah, Jeremiah) who receives revelation before judgment falls.
verses.notes: This is the theological foundation for why the three-part pattern repeats: God's character requires that He reveal His plans through prophets before executing judgment. The three angels of Rev 14 are the ultimate expression of this principle.

verses.heading: Moses/Egypt: first worship demand — 'Let my people go that they may hold a feast'
verses.kjv_text: And afterward Moses and Aaron went in, and told Pharaoh, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Let my people go, that they may hold a feast unto me in the wilderness.
verses.context: The first of the repeated worship demands. The formula 'Let my people go' uses shalach (send away) and the purpose clause 'that they may serve/worship me' uses abad (H5647). This is the gospel/worship call of the Exodus episode.
verses.notes: The first instance uses 'hold a feast' (chagag) rather than 'serve' (abad). Subsequent uses (7:16, 8:1, 9:1, 9:13, 10:3) consistently use abad — serve/worship. The core demand is for freedom to worship YHWH, paralleling the first angel's call to 'worship him that made heaven and earth.'

verses.heading: Moses/Egypt: repeated worship-call formula — 'Let my people go that they may serve me'
verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt say unto him, The LORD God of the Hebrews hath sent me unto thee, saying, Let my people go, that they may serve me in the wilderness: and, behold, hitherto thou wouldest not hear.
verses.context: The standard worship-call formula using abad (H5647). This formula repeats in Exo 8:1, 8:20, 9:1, 9:13, 10:3 — each time with escalating judgment for refusal.
verses.notes: The verb abad (H5647) is the core worship verb — translated 'serve' in the Exodus formula but fundamentally a worship term (see word study H5647-abad and TR-abad-douleuo). The repeated demand to serve/worship YHWH is the gospel call of the Exodus episode.

verses.heading: Moses/Egypt: God's gospel purpose stated — 'that my name may be declared'
verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh, and say unto him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me. For I will at this time send all my plagues upon thine heart, and upon thy servants, and upon thy people; that thou mayest know that there is none like me in all the earth. For now I will stretch out my hand, that I may smite thee and thy people with pestilence; and thou shalt be cut off from the earth. And in very deed for this cause have I raised thee up, for to shew in thee my power; and that my name may be declared throughout all the earth.
verses.context: God explicitly states the gospel purpose of the plagues: 'that my name may be declared throughout all the earth.' This is not merely punitive — it is proclamatory, matching the first angel's universal gospel commission.
verses.notes: Hebrew parser confirms: sapper (saphar, Piel infinitive construct — to recount/declare) shemi (shem + 1cs suffix — my name). The purpose of the plagues is proclamatory — declaring God's name throughout all the earth. This parallels Rev 14:6's universal scope.

verses.heading: Moses/Egypt: 'against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments'
verses.kjv_text: For I will pass through the land of Egypt this night, and will smite all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, both man and beast; and against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgment: I am the LORD.
verses.context: The definitive statement that the plagues were directed against Egypt's false worship system. The word elohey (elohiym, H430, construct plural — gods of) and shephatim (shaphat, H8199, noun mp — judgments) make this explicit.

hebrew_parsing.gloss: 'I will pass through') בְאֶֽרֶץ־מִצְרַיִם֮ (in the land of Egypt) בַּלַּ֣יְלָה הַזֶּה֒ (this night) וְהִכֵּיתִ֤י (נכה Hiphil.Perf.1s 'I will strike') כָל־בְּכֹור֙ (every firstborn) בְּאֶ֣רֶץ מִצְרַ֔יִם (in the land of Egypt) מֵאָדָ֖ם וְעַד־בְּהֵמָ֑ה (from man to beast) וּבְכָל (and against all) אֱלֹהֵ֥י (אלהים noun.mp.cst 'gods of') מִצְרַ֛יִם (Egypt) אֶֽעֱשֶׂ֥ה (עשׂה Qal.Impf.1s 'I will execute') שְׁפָטִ֖ים (שׁפט noun.mp 'judgments') אֲנִ֥י יְהוָֽה (I am YHWH)

verses.notes: Hebrew parser confirms: elohey Mitsrayim (gods of Egypt, H430 construct plural + Egypt); e'eseh shephatim (I will execute judgments, asah Qal imperfect 1s + shaphat noun mp). This verse establishes that the Exodus plagues were a systematic dismantling of the false worship system — the exposure element of the three-part pattern.

verses.heading: Moses/Egypt: confirmation — 'upon their gods also the LORD executed judgments'
verses.kjv_text: For the Egyptians buried all their firstborn, which the LORD had smitten among them: upon their gods also the LORD executed judgments.
verses.context: A retrospective confirmation of Exo 12:12 — the LORD executed judgments on Egyptian gods. This confirms the exposure-of-false-system element in the Exodus episode.
verses.notes: The identical phrase 'executed judgments' (asah shephatim) appears in both Exo 12:12 and Num 33:4, bracketing the plague narrative with the explicit statement that God judged Egypt's false worship system.

verses.heading: Elijah/Carmel: worship call — 'How long halt ye between two opinions?'
verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when Ahab saw Elijah, that Ahab said unto him, Art thou he that troubleth Israel? And he answered, I have not troubled Israel; but thou, and thy father's house, in that ye have forsaken the commandments of the LORD, and thou hast followed Baalim. Now therefore send, and gather to me all Israel unto mount Carmel, and the prophets of Baal four hundred and fifty, and the prophets of the groves four hundred, which eat at Jezebel's table. So Ahab sent unto all the children of Israel, and gathered the prophets together unto mount Carmel. And Elijah came unto all the people, and said, How long halt ye between two opinions? if the LORD be God, follow him: but if Baal, then follow him. And the people answered him not a word.
verses.context: Elijah presents the most concentrated worship call in the OT: a binary choice between YHWH and Baal. The verb pasach (H6452) — halt/limp — describes Israel's divided loyalty. Elijah first indicts Ahab for forsaking the commandments of the LORD.
verses.notes: Hebrew parser confirms: posechim (pasach, H6452, Qal participle mp) — limping/halting; YHWH (H3068); ha-elohim (H430, the God); ha-ba'al (H1168, Baal); leku (halak, Qal imperative 2mp — walk/follow). The se'ippim (H5587) — 'opinions' or 'branches' — denotes divided loyalties. This is the worship binary paralleling the first angel's call.

verses.heading: Elijah/Carmel: exposure of Baal worship as impotent
verses.kjv_text: And Elijah said unto the prophets of Baal, Choose you one bullock for yourselves, and dress it first; for ye are many; and call on the name of your gods, but put no fire under. And they took the bullock which was given them, and they dressed it, and called on the name of Baal from morning even until noon, saying, O Baal, hear us. But there was no voice, nor any that answered. And they leaped upon the altar which was made. And it came to pass at noon, that Elijah mocked them, and said, Cry aloud: for he is a god; either he is talking, or he is pursuing, or he is in a journey, or peradventure he sleepeth, and must be awaked. And they cried aloud, and cut themselves after their manner with knives and lancets, till the blood gushed out upon them. And it came to pass, when midday was past, and they prophesied until the time of the offering of the evening sacrifice, that there was neither voice, nor any to answer, nor any that regarded.
verses.context: The prolonged, humiliating failure of Baal's prophets exposes the false system. Elijah's mockery systematically dismantles Baal's claimed attributes. The prophets 'leaped upon the altar' — the verb is pasach (H6452) in Piel, the same root Elijah used for Israel's 'halting' in v.21.
verses.notes: The ironic use of pasach in v.26 (Piel intensive — they leaped/danced frenetically) echoes v.21 where the same root described Israel's 'halting' between two opinions. Elijah's mockery is devastating: 'he is talking, or he is pursuing, or he is in a journey, or he sleepeth.' The threefold repetition of 'no voice, nor any that answered' (v.26, 29) completes the exposure.

verses.heading: Elijah/Carmel: fire from heaven vindicates YHWH — 'The LORD, he is the God'
verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass at the time of the offering of the evening sacrifice, that Elijah the prophet came near, and said, LORD God of Abraham, Isaac, and of Israel, let it be known this day that thou art God in Israel, and that I am thy servant, and that I have done all these things at thy word. Hear me, O LORD, hear me, that this people may know that thou art the LORD God, and that thou hast turned their heart back again. Then the fire of the LORD fell, and consumed the burnt sacrifice, and the wood, and the stones, and the dust, and licked up the water that was in the trench. And when all the people saw it, they fell on their faces: and they said, The LORD, he is the God; the LORD, he is the God.
verses.context: YHWH answers by fire, consuming the sacrifice. The people's confession — 'YHWH hu ha-elohim' (the LORD, he is the God) — is the climactic recognition of the true God, paralleling the first angel's call to worship the Creator.
verses.notes: Hebrew parser confirms the confession: YHWH (H3068) hu (pronoun 3ms — he) ha-elohim (H430 — the God). Repeated twice for emphasis. Elijah's prayer purpose: 'that this people may know that thou art the LORD God, and that thou hast turned their heart back again' — restoration/gospel purpose.

verses.heading: Elijah/Carmel: judgment executed on false prophets
verses.kjv_text: And Elijah said unto them, Take the prophets of Baal; let not one of them escape. And they took them: and Elijah brought them down to the brook Kishon, and slew them there.
verses.context: Judgment executed on the false prophets per Deuteronomy 13:13-15, which prescribes death for those who lead Israel into false worship. This is the judgment element of the three-part pattern.
verses.notes: The execution of the false prophets follows the pattern: gospel call (v.21), exposure of false system (v.25-29), divine vindication (v.36-38), then judgment (v.40). The judgment is specifically on the agents of false worship.

verses.heading: Elijah/Carmel context: Ahab's introduction of Baal worship
verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as if it had been a light thing for him to walk in the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, that he took to wife Jezebel the daughter of Ethbaal king of the Zidonians, and went and served Baal, and worshipped him. And he reared up an altar for Baal in the house of Baal, which he had built in Samaria. And Ahab made a grove; and Ahab did more to provoke the LORD God of Israel to anger than all the kings of Israel that were before him.
verses.context: The immediate background for the Carmel confrontation: Ahab married Jezebel and imported Baal worship into Israel. He 'served Baal, and worshipped him' — the verb abad (H5647) and shachah (H7812), the core worship vocabulary.
verses.notes: Both key worship verbs appear: abad (served) and shachah (worshipped). This establishes that the Carmel confrontation was specifically about worship — the same issue at the heart of the first angel's message.

verses.heading: Josiah: discovery of the book of the law — gospel/covenant call rediscovered
verses.kjv_text: And Hilkiah the high priest said unto Shaphan the scribe, I have found the book of the law in the house of the LORD. And Hilkiah gave the book to Shaphan, and he read it. And Shaphan the scribe came to the king, and brought the king word again, and said, Thy servants have gathered the money that was found in the house, and have delivered it into the hand of them that do the work, that have the oversight of the house of the LORD. And Shaphan the scribe shewed the king, saying, Hilkiah the priest hath delivered me a book. And Shaphan read it before the king. And it came to pass, when the king had heard the words of the book of the law, that he rent his clothes. And the king commanded Hilkiah the priest, and Ahikam the son of Shaphan, and Achbor the son of Michaiah, and Shaphan the scribe, and Asahiah a servant of the king's, saying, Go ye, inquire of the LORD for me, and for the people, and for all Judah, concerning the words of this book that is found: for great is the wrath of the LORD that is kindled against us, because our fathers have not hearkened unto the words of this book, to do according unto all that which is written concerning us.
verses.context: The discovery of the book of the law (towrah, H8451) is the gospel/covenant call element. Josiah's response — rending his clothes — shows genuine repentance. He immediately recognizes that the nation has broken covenant.
verses.notes: The sepher ha-towrah (book of the law, H8451) functions as the gospel call — the rediscovery of God's covenant requirements. Josiah's response is exemplary: he rent his clothes (mourning/repentance), inquired of the LORD, and acknowledged corporate guilt.

verses.heading: Josiah: Huldah's judgment prophecy — wrath kindled despite reform
verses.kjv_text: And she said unto them, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Tell the man that sent you to me, Thus saith the LORD, Behold, I will bring evil upon this place, and upon the inhabitants thereof, even all the words of the book which the king of Judah hath read: Because they have forsaken me, and have burned incense unto other gods, that they might provoke me to anger with all the works of their hands; therefore my wrath shall be kindled against this place, and shall not be quenched.
verses.context: Huldah's prophecy is the judgment warning element. Despite Josiah's reform, corporate judgment remains fixed because of systemic apostasy. The word chemah (H2534 — wrath/fury) appears in 'my wrath shall be kindled.'
verses.notes: Hebrew parser confirms: chamati (chemah, H2534, feminine noun + 1cs suffix — my wrath/fury); nitstsethah (yatsath, Niphal perfect 3fs — shall be kindled); lo tikhbeh (kabah, Qal imperfect 3fs — shall not be quenched). This is the judgment element — irrevocable despite personal repentance.

verses.heading: Josiah: covenant renewal and systematic exposure/destruction of idolatry
verses.kjv_text: And the king stood by a pillar, and made a covenant before the LORD, to walk after the LORD, and to keep his commandments and his testimonies and his statutes with all their heart and all their soul, to perform the words of this covenant that were written in this book. And all the people stood to the covenant. And the king commanded Hilkiah the high priest, and the priests of the second order, and the keepers of the door, to bring forth out of the temple of the LORD all the vessels that were made for Baal, and for the grove, and for all the host of heaven: and he burned them without Jerusalem in the fields of Kidron, and carried the ashes of them unto Bethel. And he put down the idolatrous priests, whom the kings of Judah had ordained to burn incense in the high places in the cities of Judah, and in the places round about Jerusalem; them also that burned incense unto Baal, to the sun, and to the moon, and to the planets, and to all the host of heaven.
verses.context: The covenant renewal (v.3) is the gospel response. The systematic destruction of idolatry (v.4-5ff) is the most detailed exposure of false worship in the OT — Baal vessels, grove/Asherah, host of heaven, idolatrous priests, sun worship.
verses.notes: The exposure is exhaustive: Baal (H1168), grove/Asherah (H842), host of heaven, sun, moon, planets. This is the most thorough dismantling of a false worship system in any OT episode. Note the sun worship element (v.5, 11) — relevant to Bohr's claim about Baal as 'sun god,' though in the 1 Kings 18 context Baal is presented as storm/rain god.

verses.heading: Josiah: the most thoroughgoing reform — 'like unto him was there no king'
verses.kjv_text: Moreover the workers with familiar spirits, and the wizards, and the images, and the idols, and all the abominations that were spied in the land of Judah and in Jerusalem, did Josiah put away, that he might perform the words of the law which were written in the book that Hilkiah the priest found in the house of the LORD. And like unto him was there no king before him, that turned to the LORD with all his heart, and with all his soul, and with all his might, according to all the law of Moses; neither after him arose there any like him.
verses.context: The superlative commendation: no king before or after Josiah turned to the LORD with such wholehearted devotion. Yet the next verses (23:26-27) reveal that corporate judgment was not averted — paralleling how Rev 14's messages save individuals while corporate Babylon still falls.
verses.notes: The phrase 'with all his heart, and with all his soul, and with all his might' echoes the Shema (Deut 6:5). Despite this exemplary response, 2 Ki 23:26-27 states that 'the LORD turned not from the fierceness of his great wrath.' Individual reform cannot avert corporate judgment.

verses.heading: Jeremiah: temple sermon — gospel call to amend ways
verses.kjv_text: The word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD, saying, Stand in the gate of the LORD'S house, and proclaim there this word, and say, Hear the word of the LORD, all ye of Judah, that enter in at these gates to worship the LORD. Thus saith the LORD of hosts, the God of Israel, Amend your ways and your doings, and I will cause you to dwell in this place. Trust ye not in lying words, saying, The temple of the LORD, The temple of the LORD, The temple of the LORD, are these. For if ye throughly amend your ways and your doings; if ye throughly execute judgment between a man and his neighbour; If ye oppress not the stranger, the fatherless, and the widow, and shed not innocent blood in this place, neither walk after other gods to your hurt: Then will I cause you to dwell in this place, in the land that I gave to your fathers, for ever and ever.
verses.context: Jeremiah's temple sermon is the gospel/worship call element. God calls for genuine repentance: 'Amend your ways.' The conditional promise — 'if ye throughly amend... I will cause you to dwell' — is the gospel offer.
verses.notes: The call is addressed to those who 'enter in at these gates to worship the LORD' — the audience includes worshipers. The problem is false confidence in the temple ('The temple of the LORD' repeated three times) — a false system exposed.

verses.heading: Jeremiah: exposure of false confidence in temple system — 'den of robbers'
verses.kjv_text: Behold, ye trust in lying words, that cannot profit. Will ye steal, murder, and commit adultery, and swear falsely, and burn incense unto Baal, and walk after other gods whom ye know not; And come and stand before me in this house, which is called by my name, and say, We are delivered to do all these abominations? Is this house, which is called by my name, become a den of robbers in your eyes? Behold, even I have seen it, saith the LORD.
verses.context: The exposure element: false confidence in the temple institution while practicing every form of covenant violation. The list of sins matches the Decalogue violations. 'Den of robbers' — later quoted by Jesus (Matt 21:13).
verses.notes: The exposure is systematic: steal (8th commandment), murder (6th), adultery (7th), swear falsely (9th), burn incense unto Baal (1st/2nd), walk after other gods (1st). This is a covenant-lawsuit indictment — rib language applied to the temple worshipers.

verses.heading: Jeremiah: 23 years of prophetic warning — gospel call to repent
verses.kjv_text: The word that came to Jeremiah concerning all the people of Judah in the fourth year of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah king of Judah, that was the first year of Nebuchadrezzar king of Babylon; The which Jeremiah the prophet spake unto all the people of Judah, and to all the inhabitants of Jerusalem, saying, From the thirteenth year of Josiah the son of Amon king of Judah, even unto this day, that is the three and twentieth year, the word of the LORD hath come unto me, and I have spoken unto you, rising early and speaking; but ye have not hearkened. And the LORD hath sent unto you all his servants the prophets, rising early and sending them; but ye have not hearkened, nor inclined your ear to hear. They said, Turn ye again now every one from his evil way, and from the evil of your doings, and dwell in the land that the LORD hath given unto you and to your fathers for ever and ever: And go not after other gods to serve them, and to worship them, and provoke me not to anger with the works of your hands; and I will do you no hurt. Yet ye have not hearkened unto me, saith the LORD; that ye might provoke me to anger with the works of your hands to your own hurt.
verses.context: Jeremiah summarizes 23 years of gospel call: 'Turn ye again now every one from his evil way.' God sent 'all his servants the prophets' — the Amos 3:7 principle in sustained application. The worship vocabulary appears: 'serve them, and worship them' (abad and shachah).
verses.notes: Both worship verbs appear: abad (H5647 — serve) and shachah (H7812 — worship). The 23-year duration parallels Noah's 120-year period — extended gospel opportunity before judgment. The phrase 'rising early and sending' emphasizes God's persistent effort.

verses.heading: Jeremiah: 'Flee out of Babylon' — direct source for Rev 18:4
verses.kjv_text: Flee out of the midst of Babylon, and deliver every man his soul: be not cut off in her iniquity; for this is the time of the LORD'S vengeance; he will render unto her a recompence. Babylon hath been a golden cup in the LORD'S hand, that made all the earth drunken: the nations have drunken of her wine; therefore the nations are mad. Babylon is suddenly fallen and destroyed: howl for her; take balm for her pain, if so be she may be healed. We would have healed Babylon, but she is not healed: forsake her, and let us go every one into his own country: for her judgment reacheth unto heaven, and is lifted up even to the skies.
verses.context: The primary OT source text for Rev 18:4. The 'flee out' (nus, H5127) and 'deliver every man his soul' (malat, Piel imperative) are the 'come out' call. Jer 51:7 ('golden cup that made all the earth drunken') is the direct source for Rev 14:8 and 17:4. Jer 51:8 ('Babylon is suddenly fallen and destroyed') is the source for Rev 14:8's 'Babylon is fallen, is fallen.'

hebrew_parsing.gloss: 'flee!') מִתֹּ֣וךְ (from the midst of) בָּבֶ֗ל (Babylon) וּמַלְּטוּ֙ (מלט Piel.Impv.2mp 'deliver/escape!') אִ֣ישׁ (each man) נַפְשֹׁ֔ו (his soul) אַל תִּדַּ֖מּוּ (do not be silent/rest) בַּעֲוֹנָ֑הּ (in her iniquity) כִּי֩ (for) עֵ֨ת (time of) נְקָמָ֥ה (vengeance) הִיא֙ (it is) לַֽיהוָ֔ה (to YHWH) גְּמ֕וּל (recompense) ה֥וּא (he) מְשַׁלֵּ֖ם (שׁלם Piel.Ptcp 'repaying') לָֽהּ (to her)

verses.notes: Hebrew parser confirms: nusu (nus, H5127, Qal imperative 2mp — flee); malletu (malat, Piel imperative 2mp — escape/deliver); eth neqamah (time of vengeance). Jer 51:7 uses the cup imagery that becomes central to Revelation's Babylon: 'golden cup in the LORD's hand that made all the earth drunken.' The lexical chain: Jer 51:6-9 → Rev 14:8, 17:4, 18:4.

verses.heading: Jeremiah: judgment on Bel/Babylon and 'come out of her, my people'
verses.kjv_text: And I will punish Bel in Babylon, and I will bring forth out of his mouth that which he hath swallowed up: and the nations shall not flow together any more unto him: yea, the wall of Babylon shall fall. My people, go ye out of the midst of her, and deliver ye every man his soul from the fierce anger of the LORD.
verses.context: God punishes Bel (the Babylonian form of Baal, H1078) and commands: 'My people, go ye out of the midst of her.' This combines all three elements: judgment on false god (exposure), coming-out call (gospel), and fierce anger of the LORD (judgment warning).
verses.notes: Bel (H1078) is the Babylonian form of Baal (H1168). The phrase 'My people, go ye out' (ammi tse'u) uses yatsa (H3318, Qal imperative 2mp) — the exact verb that becomes exerchomai (G1831) in Rev 18:4. This is the most direct textual source for the NT 'come out' call.

verses.heading: Covenant lawsuit (rib): opening formula — 'the LORD hath a controversy'
verses.kjv_text: Hear the word of the LORD, ye children of Israel: for the LORD hath a controversy with the inhabitants of the land, because there is no truth, nor mercy, nor knowledge of God in the land.
verses.context: The classic covenant lawsuit (rib, H7378) opening. God brings a formal legal complaint against Israel. The indictment: no truth (emeth), no mercy (chesed), no knowledge of God (da'ath elohim). This maps to the three-part pattern: God's case is heard (gospel context), violations are exposed, judgment follows.

hebrew_parsing.gloss: 'hear!') דְבַר (word of) יְהוָ֖ה (YHWH) בְּנֵ֣י (sons of) יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל (Israel) כִּ֣י (for) רִ֤יב (ריב noun.ms 'lawsuit/controversy') לַֽיהוָה֙ (to YHWH) עִם (with) יֹושְׁבֵ֣י (ישׁב Qal.Ptcp.mp.cst 'inhabitants of') הָאָ֔רֶץ (the land) כִּ֠י (because) אֵין אֱמֶ֧ת (no truth) וְֽאֵין חֶ֛סֶד (and no mercy) וְאֵֽין דַּ֥עַת אֱלֹהִ֖ים (and no knowledge of God) בָּאָֽרֶץ (in the land)

verses.notes: Hebrew parser confirms: riyb (H7378, noun ms — law-case/controversy); emeth (trustworthiness); chesed (loyalty); da'ath elohim (knowledge of God). The rib structure has three elements matching the three angels: (1) recall of covenant obligations = gospel call, (2) indictment of violations = exposure, (3) announcement of consequences = judgment warning.

verses.heading: Covenant lawsuit (rib): mountains as witnesses — formal legal proceeding
verses.kjv_text: Hear ye now what the LORD saith; Arise, contend thou before the mountains, and let the hills hear thy voice. Hear ye, O mountains, the LORD'S controversy, and ye strong foundations of the earth: for the LORD hath a controversy with his people, and he will plead with Israel.
verses.context: The most formal covenant lawsuit passage. Mountains and foundations of the earth are summoned as witnesses — recalling the creation witnesses of the original covenant (Deut 32:1). The verb yithvakkach (yakach, Hithpael — 'he will plead/argue') intensifies the legal proceeding.
verses.notes: Hebrew parser confirms: riyb (H7378, noun ms construct — law-case of) YHWH; yithvakkach (yakach, Hithpael imperfect 3ms — he will argue/plead). The mountains as witnesses parallel the heavenly court setting. Micah 6:3-5 continues with God's defense (gospel recall), 6:6-8 with the proper response, establishing the three-part pattern within the rib structure.

verses.heading: Covenant lawsuit: heaven and earth as witnesses — exposure of rebellion
verses.kjv_text: Hear, O heavens, and give ear, O earth: for the LORD hath spoken, I have nourished and brought up children, and they have rebelled against me. The ox knoweth his owner, and the ass his master's crib: but Israel doth not know, my people doth not consider. Ah sinful nation, a people laden with iniquity, a seed of evildoers, children that are corrupters: they have forsaken the LORD, they have provoked the Holy One of Israel unto anger, they are gone away backward.
verses.context: Isaiah's opening oracle follows the covenant lawsuit form: heaven and earth summoned as witnesses (Deut 32:1), God's nurturing recalled (gospel element), Israel's rebellion exposed (exposure element). The indictment: they have forsaken the LORD.
verses.notes: The covenant lawsuit structure is clear: (1) summons to heaven and earth as witnesses (v.2a), (2) recall of God's grace — 'I have nourished and brought up children' = gospel element, (3) indictment of rebellion = exposure, followed by judgment warning in v.18-20.

verses.heading: Covenant lawsuit: gospel offer and judgment alternative
verses.kjv_text: Come now, and let us reason together, saith the LORD: though your sins be as scarlet, they shall be as white as snow; though they be red like crimson, they shall be as wool. If ye be willing and obedient, ye shall eat the good of the land: But if ye refuse and rebel, ye shall be devoured with the sword: for the mouth of the LORD hath spoken it.
verses.context: Following the covenant lawsuit indictment (1:2-15), God offers the gospel: 'Come now, and let us reason together... your sins shall be as white as snow.' Then the binary outcome: willing and obedient = blessing; refuse and rebel = judgment by sword.
verses.notes: This passage shows all three elements within the rib framework: (1) gospel offer — forgiveness despite scarlet sins, (2) the indictment has already been delivered (v.2-15), (3) judgment alternative — 'ye shall be devoured with the sword.' The binary structure parallels the first angel's worship call and the third angel's judgment warning.

verses.heading: Jeremiah's covenant lawsuit: 'I will yet plead with you'
verses.kjv_text: Wherefore I will yet plead with you, saith the LORD, and with your children's children will I plead.
verses.context: Jeremiah uses the rib verb (H7378) — 'I will yet plead (ariyb) with you.' The word 'yet' (od) shows that God's controversy is ongoing, extending even to future generations. This connects the rib tradition to Jeremiah's ministry.
verses.notes: The verb ariyb (riyb, H7378, Qal imperfect 1cs — I will contend/plead) confirms Jeremiah's ministry operates within the covenant lawsuit framework. The extension to 'your children's children' shows the multigenerational scope of the prophetic warning.

verses.heading: Come-out tradition: 'Remove out of the midst of Babylon'
verses.kjv_text: Remove out of the midst of Babylon, and go forth out of the land of the Chaldeans, and be as the he goats before the flocks.
verses.context: Part of the 'come out' vocabulary chain. The verb yatsa (H3318) appears in 'go forth.' This text is part of the Jeremiah 50-51 oracle against Babylon that forms the primary source for Revelation 17-18.
verses.notes: The 'come out' chain: Jer 50:8 ('Remove out... go forth') → Jer 51:6 ('Flee out') → Jer 51:45 ('go ye out') → Isa 48:20 ('Go ye forth of Babylon') → Isa 52:11 ('Depart ye... go ye out') → Rev 18:4 ('Come out of her').

verses.heading: Come-out tradition: 'Go ye forth of Babylon, flee ye from the Chaldeans'
verses.kjv_text: Go ye forth of Babylon, flee ye from the Chaldeans, with a voice of singing declare ye, tell this, utter it even to the end of the earth; say ye, The LORD hath redeemed his servant Jacob.
verses.context: Isaiah's 'come out' call adds the gospel element: 'with a voice of singing declare ye... The LORD hath redeemed.' This is not merely escape from judgment but joyful proclamation of redemption — combining the 'come out' call with gospel celebration.
verses.notes: The verb yatsa (H3318) appears in 'Go ye forth' (tse'u). Isaiah adds the redemption/gospel dimension: the departure from Babylon is accompanied by singing and declaring God's redemption. This enriches the 'come out' tradition beyond mere escape.

verses.heading: Come-out tradition: 'Depart ye, go ye out from thence'
verses.kjv_text: Depart ye, depart ye, go ye out from thence, touch no unclean thing; go ye out of the midst of her; be ye clean, that bear the vessels of the LORD.
verses.context: The doubled imperative 'Depart ye, depart ye' intensifies the urgency. The purity requirement ('touch no unclean thing, be ye clean') adds a holiness dimension to the 'come out' call. Paul quotes this in 2 Cor 6:17.
verses.notes: The verb yatsa (H3318) appears twice: 'go ye out from thence' and 'go ye out of the midst of her.' The purity element parallels Rev 18:4's 'that ye be not partakers of her sins.' The separation is both physical and spiritual.

verses.heading: Watchman principle: warning trumpet before judgment
verses.kjv_text: If when he seeth the sword come upon the land, he blow the trumpet, and warn the people; Then whosoever heareth the sound of the trumpet, and taketh not warning; if the sword come, and take him away, his blood shall be upon his own head. He heard the sound of the trumpet, and took not warning; his blood shall be upon him. But he that taketh warning shall deliver his soul.
verses.context: The watchman principle establishes the theological basis for warning before judgment. The watchman's trumpet parallels the angel's 'loud voice' in Rev 14. Those who heed the warning 'deliver their soul' — those who reject it bear their own blood.
verses.notes: This passage is the theological foundation for the entire warning-before-judgment pattern. The three elements are present: (1) the watchman blows the trumpet = gospel/warning proclamation, (2) the danger is identified = exposure, (3) the consequence for ignoring is death = judgment. The phrase 'deliver his soul' (malat nephesh) connects to Jer 51:6 ('deliver every man his soul').

verses.heading: Sodom's sins enumerated: pride, fullness, idleness, neglect of poor
verses.kjv_text: Behold, this was the iniquity of thy sister Sodom, pride, fulness of bread, and abundance of idleness was in her and in her daughters, neither did she strengthen the hand of the poor and needy.
verses.context: Ezekiel provides the fullest enumeration of Sodom's sins: pride, abundance, idleness, and neglect of the poor. This fills out the 'exposure of false system' element for the Sodom episode beyond sexual sin alone.
verses.notes: Sodom's sins include social injustice (neglect of the poor), pride, and complacency — a broader indictment than just the sexual sin of Gen 19:4-5. This shows the 'false system' exposed was comprehensive moral corruption.

verses.heading: Jesus connects Noah's time to end-time judgment — typological link
verses.kjv_text: But as the days of Noe were, so shall also the coming of the Son of man be. For as in the days that were before the flood they were eating and drinking, marrying and giving in marriage, until the day that Noe entered into the ark, And knew not until the flood came, and took them all away; so shall also the coming of the Son of man be.
verses.context: Jesus explicitly parallels Noah's time with the end-time, confirming the typological link between the Noah episode and the three angels' messages era. The emphasis is on unexpected judgment — 'knew not until the flood came.'
verses.notes: Jesus validates the Noah-to-end-time typological connection. The pattern Jesus identifies: warning given (Noah preached), normal life continued in defiance of warning (eating, drinking, marrying), judgment fell unexpectedly. This is the three-part pattern applied eschatologically.

verses.heading: Jesus connects both Noah and Lot/Sodom to end-time — double typological link
verses.kjv_text: And as it was in the days of Noe, so shall it be also in the days of the Son of man. They did eat, they drank, they married wives, they were given in marriage, until the day that Noe entered into the ark, and the flood came, and destroyed them all. Likewise also as it was in the days of Lot; they did eat, they drank, they bought, they sold, they planted, they builded; But the same day that Lot went out of Sodom it rained fire and brimstone from heaven, and destroyed them all.
verses.context: Jesus pairs both Noah and Lot/Sodom as end-time types. This confirms that both episodes — the flood and Sodom's fire — are structural precedents for end-time judgment. The 'same day' timing emphasizes that judgment falls when the righteous are extracted.
verses.notes: Jesus pairs two of the six OT episodes under study as direct types of end-time events. The pattern in both: warning given, daily life continues in defiance, righteous extracted, judgment falls the same day. This validates the three-part pattern as a recurring divine methodology.

verses.heading: Sodom as example — God delivers the godly while judging the ungodly
verses.kjv_text: And turning the cities of Sodom and Gomorrha into ashes condemned them with an overthrow, making them an ensample unto those that after should live ungodly; And delivered just Lot, vexed with the filthy conversation of the wicked: (For that righteous man dwelling among them, in seeing and hearing, vexed his righteous soul from day to day with their unlawful deeds;) The Lord knoweth how to deliver the godly out of temptations, and to reserve the unjust unto the day of judgment to be punished:
verses.context: Peter uses Sodom as a paradigm for how God operates: He delivers the godly (Lot) while reserving the unjust for judgment. This dual action — salvation and judgment — is exactly the three-part pattern: gospel (deliverance), exposure (unlawful deeds), judgment (day of judgment).
verses.notes: The principle in v.9 — 'The Lord knoweth how to deliver the godly out of temptations, and to reserve the unjust unto the day of judgment' — summarizes the three-part pattern's dual outcome: salvation for those who heed, judgment for those who reject.

verses.heading: Sodom as 'example, suffering the vengeance of eternal fire'
verses.kjv_text: Even as Sodom and Gomorrha, and the cities about them in like manner, giving themselves over to fornication, and going after strange flesh, are set forth for an example, suffering the vengeance of eternal fire.
verses.context: Jude uses Sodom as an 'example' (deigma) of divine judgment by fire. The fire-and-brimstone language connects directly to Rev 14:10's third angel warning.
verses.notes: The word 'example' (deigma) confirms that Sodom's judgment is paradigmatic — it establishes a pattern that repeats. The 'eternal fire' connects to Rev 14:10 (fire and brimstone in the presence of the Lamb).

verses.heading: Enoch's pre-flood judgment prophecy — warning tradition before Noah
verses.kjv_text: And Enoch also, the seventh from Adam, prophesied of these, saying, Behold, the Lord cometh with ten thousands of his saints, To execute judgment upon all, and to convince all that are ungodly among them of all their ungodly deeds which they have ungodly committed, and of all their hard speeches which ungodly sinners have spoken against him.
verses.context: Jude cites Enoch's prophecy as predating Noah's ministry — establishing that the warning-before-judgment tradition extends back to 'the seventh from Adam.' This pushes the three-part pattern further back in history.
verses.notes: Enoch prophesied judgment 'upon all' for 'ungodly deeds' — the exposure and judgment elements are explicit. The fourfold repetition of 'ungodly' (asebeia/asebes) emphasizes the moral exposure. This extends the pattern before Noah's time.

verses.heading: Noah's ark and God's longsuffering — gospel patience before judgment
verses.kjv_text: Which sometime were disobedient, when once the longsuffering of God waited in the days of Noah, while the ark was a preparing, wherein few, that is, eight souls were saved by water.
verses.context: Peter emphasizes God's longsuffering (makrothymia) — He waited patiently during the ark's construction. The building period was itself a gospel invitation: time to repent. Only eight souls responded.
verses.notes: The 'longsuffering of God' parallels the 120-year probationary period (Gen 6:3) and connects to 2 Pet 3:9 ('not willing that any should perish'). The few who were saved (eight) anticipates the 'patience of the saints' (Rev 14:12) — the faithful remnant who heed the warning.

verses.heading: Song of Moses at Red Sea — judgment and deliverance celebrated
verses.kjv_text: Then sang Moses and the children of Israel this song unto the LORD, and spake, saying, I will sing unto the LORD, for he hath triumphed gloriously: the horse and his rider hath he thrown into the sea. The LORD is my strength and song, and he is become my salvation: he is my God, and I will prepare him an habitation; my father's God, and I will exalt him.
verses.context: The Song of Moses celebrates God's judgment on Egypt and Israel's deliverance — both elements of the three-part pattern. Rev 15:3-4 explicitly combines this song with 'the song of the Lamb,' creating the Exodus-to-Revelation typological bridge.
verses.notes: This song is the post-judgment celebration after the Exodus three-part pattern has been completed: worship call (let my people go to serve), exposure (judgment on Egyptian gods), judgment (Red Sea). Rev 15:3 explicitly references this song in the end-time setting.

verses.heading: Moses/Egypt: intensified worship demand — 'How long wilt thou refuse to humble thyself?'
verses.kjv_text: And Moses and Aaron came in unto Pharaoh, and said unto him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, How long wilt thou refuse to humble thyself before me? let my people go, that they may serve me.
verses.context: The most intense form of the worship-call formula. God now asks 'How long?' — the same language Elijah uses at Carmel (1 Ki 18:21 'How long halt ye?'). The demand to 'humble thyself before me' (anah, H6031) adds a personal dimension to the worship call.
verses.notes: The 'How long?' formula connects the Moses/Egypt episode to the Elijah/Carmel episode. Both prophets challenge the audience's persistent refusal to acknowledge YHWH. The verb abad (H5647, 'serve me') continues the worship-call vocabulary.

verses.heading: Song of Moses references Sodom — Sodom judgment applied to Israel's future
verses.kjv_text: For their vine is of the vine of Sodom, and of the fields of Gomorrah: their grapes are grapes of gall, their clusters are bitter:
verses.context: Moses's Song (Deut 32) applies Sodom language to Israel itself — those who abandon YHWH become like Sodom. This connects the Abraham/Sodom episode to Israel's own future apostasy, showing the three-part pattern as applicable beyond pagans.
verses.notes: The Sodom typology is reapplied to God's own people — those who forsake the covenant become like Sodom. This prepares for Isaiah's use of the same typology (Isa 1:10) and shows that the three-part pattern is not merely about external enemies but about covenant unfaithfulness.

verses.heading: Isaiah calls Judah's leaders 'rulers of Sodom' — Sodom typology reapplied
verses.kjv_text: Hear the word of the LORD, ye rulers of Sodom; give ear unto the law of our God, ye people of Gomorrah.
verses.context: Isaiah addresses Judah's leaders as 'rulers of Sodom' — applying the Sodom judgment type to God's own people. This is within the Isaiah 1 covenant lawsuit (rib) passage, combining the Sodom episode with the rib tradition.
verses.notes: The Sodom typology applied to Judah's leaders within a covenant lawsuit context (Isa 1:2-20). The combination of Sodom language with rib structure shows how the three-part pattern operates across multiple OT episodes simultaneously. The command to 'hear the word of the LORD' and 'give ear unto the law' is the gospel/covenant call.

verses.heading: Jeremiah exposes idols as powerless — systematic exposure of false worship
verses.kjv_text: Hear ye the word which the LORD speaketh unto you, O house of Israel: Thus saith the LORD, Learn not the way of the heathen, and be not dismayed at the signs of heaven; for the heathen are dismayed at them. For the customs of the people are vain: for one cutteth a tree out of the forest, the work of the hands of the workman, with the axe. They deck it with silver and with gold; they fasten it with nails and with hammers, that it move not. They are upright as the palm tree, but speak not: they must needs be borne, because they cannot go. Be not afraid of them; for they cannot do evil, neither also is it in them to do good.
verses.context: Jeremiah systematically exposes the manufacture and impotence of idols — they are cut from trees, decorated with metal, nailed in place, cannot speak, cannot walk, cannot do good or evil. This parallels Elijah's exposure of Baal at Carmel and Isaiah 44's idol-maker satire.
verses.notes: The exposure is methodical: raw material (tree), fabrication (workman, axe), decoration (silver, gold), physical limitations (cannot speak, cannot walk, cannot act). 'Be not afraid of them' — they are powerless, unlike YHWH. This is the exposure element in Jeremiah's ministry.

verses.heading: Jeremiah: 'Babylon hath been a golden cup' — direct source for Rev 14:8
verses.kjv_text: Babylon hath been a golden cup in the LORD'S hand, that made all the earth drunken: the nations have drunken of her wine; therefore the nations are mad.
verses.context: The primary OT source for Rev 14:8's imagery. Babylon as a golden cup intoxicating all nations. The second angel's message ('she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication') draws directly from this verse.
verses.notes: Lexical chain: Jer 51:7 'golden cup... made all the earth drunken... nations have drunken of her wine' → Rev 14:8 'she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication' → Rev 17:4 'golden cup in her hand full of abominations.' The cup-of-wrath tradition (chemah, H2534) links through LXX thumos (G2372) to Rev 14:10.

verses.heading: Josiah reform: personal seeking of God from youth — gospel-response element
verses.kjv_text: Josiah was eight years old when he began to reign, and he reigned in Jerusalem one and thirty years. And he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, and walked in the ways of David his father, and declined neither to the right hand, nor to the left. For in the eighth year of his reign, while he was yet young, he began to seek after the God of David his father: and in the twelfth year he began to purge Judah and Jerusalem from the high places, and the groves, and the carved images, and the molten images. And they brake down the altars of Baalim in his presence; and the images, that were on high above them, he cut down; and the groves, and the carved images, and the molten images, he brake in pieces, and made dust of them, and strowed it upon the graves of them that had sacrificed unto them.
verses.context: The Chronicles account provides the extended timeline: Josiah began seeking God at age 16 (8th year of reign) and began purging idolatry at age 20 (12th year). The gospel-response element: personal seeking of God preceded the public reform.
verses.notes: Chronicles adds detail not in Kings: the Baalim altars were broken, images cut down, carved/molten images smashed to dust and scattered on the graves of those who sacrificed to them. The dust-on-graves detail intensifies the exposure/dismantling element.

verses.heading: Josiah: expanded account of law discovery and national covenant renewal
verses.kjv_text: And when they brought out the money that was brought into the house of the LORD, Hilkiah the priest found a book of the law of the LORD given by Moses. And Hilkiah answered and said to Shaphan the scribe, I have found the book of the law in the house of the LORD. And Hilkiah delivered the book to Shaphan. And Shaphan carried the book to the king, and brought the king word back again, saying, All that was committed to thy servants, they do it. And they have gathered together the money that was found in the house of the LORD, and have delivered it into the hand of the overseers, and to the hand of the workmen. Then Shaphan the scribe told the king, saying, Hilkiah the priest hath given me a book. And Shaphan read it before the king. And it came to pass, when the king had heard the words of the law, that he rent his clothes. And the king commanded Hilkiah, and Ahikam the son of Shaphan, and Abdon the son of Micah, and Shaphan the scribe, and Asaiah a servant of the king's, saying, Go, inquire of the LORD for me, and for them that are left in Israel and in Judah, concerning the words of the book that is found: for great is the wrath of the LORD that is poured out upon us, because our fathers have not kept the word of the LORD, to do after all that is written in this book. And Hilkiah, and they that the king had appointed, went to Huldah the prophetess, the wife of Shallum the son of Tikvath, the son of Hasrah, keeper of the wardrobe; (now she dwelt in Jerusalem in the college:) and they spake to her to that effect. And she answered them, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Tell ye the man that sent you to me, Thus saith the LORD, Behold, I will bring evil upon this place, and upon the inhabitants thereof, even all the curses that are written in the book which they have read before the king of Judah: Because they have forsaken me, and have burned incense unto other gods, that they might provoke me to anger with all the works of their hands; therefore my wrath shall be poured out upon this place, and shall not be quenched. And as for the king of Judah, who sent you to inquire of the LORD, so shall ye say unto him, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel concerning the words which thou hast heard; Because thine heart was tender, and thou didst humble thyself before God, when thou heardest his words against this place, and against the inhabitants thereof, and humbledst thyself before me, and didst rend thy clothes, and weep before me; I have even heard thee also, saith the LORD. Behold, I will gather thee to thy fathers, and thou shalt be gathered to thy grave in peace, neither shall thine eyes see all the evil that I will bring upon this place, and upon the inhabitants of the same. So they brought the king word again. Then the king sent and gathered together all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem. And the king went up into the house of the LORD, and all the men of Judah, and the inhabitants of Jerusalem, and the priests, and the Levites, and all the people, great and small: and he read in their ears all the words of the book of the covenant that was found in the house of the LORD. And the king stood in his place, and made a covenant before the LORD, to walk after the LORD, and to keep his commandments, and his testimonies, and his statutes, with all his heart, and with all his soul, to perform the words of the covenant which are written in this book. And he caused all that were present in Jerusalem and Benjamin to stand to it. And the inhabitants of Jerusalem did according to the covenant of God, the God of their fathers. And Josiah took away all the abominations out of all the countries that pertained to the children of Israel, and made all that were present in Israel to serve, even to serve the LORD their God. And all his days they departed not from following the LORD, the God of their fathers.
verses.context: The expanded Chronicles account of Josiah's reform includes Huldah's dual prophecy (judgment on the nation, personal exemption for Josiah), the covenant renewal, and the national reform. All three elements of the pattern are present: gospel/covenant call (law discovered), exposure (idolatry purged), judgment warning (Huldah's prophecy).
verses.notes: Chronicles adds that Huldah's prophecy uses 'all the curses that are written in the book' (v.24) — connecting the judgment to specific covenant curse language from Deuteronomy 28. The covenant renewal (v.31) includes 'commandments, testimonies, and statutes' — the full torah vocabulary. Despite the reform, 'my wrath shall be poured out' (v.25) remains irrevocable for the nation.

verses.heading: Lamentations for Josiah — despite reform, judgment could not be averted
verses.kjv_text: And Jeremiah lamented for Josiah: and all the singing men and the singing women spake of Josiah in their lamentations to this day, and made them an ordinance in Israel: and, behold, they are written in the lamentations.
verses.context: Jeremiah lamented for Josiah — connecting the Josiah episode to the Jeremiah episode. Josiah's death in battle (despite his righteousness) confirmed that corporate judgment was not averted by individual reform.
verses.notes: The transition from Josiah's reform to Jeremiah's ministry is explicit: Jeremiah lamented for Josiah. After Josiah's death, the nation reverted to apostasy, and the judgment Huldah prophesied was fulfilled through Babylon. The three-part pattern in Josiah's case shows that even the most thoroughgoing reform cannot avert corporate judgment when the people remain uncommitted.

verses.heading: Sodom's sins enumerated in full — pride, abundance, idleness, neglect of poor
verses.kjv_text: And thine elder sister is Samaria, she and her daughters that dwell at thy left hand: and thy younger sister, that dwelleth at thy right hand, is Sodom and her daughters. Yet hast thou not walked after their ways, nor done after their abominations: but, as if that were a very little thing, thou wast corrupted more than they in all thy ways. As I live, saith the Lord GOD, Sodom thy sister hath not done, she nor her daughters, as thou hast done, thou and thy daughters. Behold, this was the iniquity of thy sister Sodom, pride, fulness of bread, and abundance of idleness was in her and in her daughters, neither did she strengthen the hand of the poor and needy.
verses.context: Ezekiel provides the fullest enumeration of Sodom's sins: pride, abundance of food, idle complacency, and failure to care for the poor. This broadens the exposure element beyond the sexual sin of Gen 19:4-5. Ezekiel also declares that Jerusalem was worse than Sodom.
verses.notes: The exposure element for Sodom is comprehensive: social injustice (neglect of poor), pride, complacency, and abundance leading to moral decay. Ezekiel's comparison — Jerusalem is worse than Sodom — shows the three-part pattern applying to God's own people, not just external enemies. This is relevant to how the three angels' messages address God's professed people.

verses.heading: Josiah's systematic destruction of idolatrous apparatus — detailed exposure/purging
verses.kjv_text: And the king commanded Hilkiah the high priest, and the priests of the second order, and the keepers of the door, to bring forth out of the temple of the LORD all the vessels that were made for Baal, and for the grove, and for all the host of heaven: and he burned them without Jerusalem in the fields of Kidron, and carried the ashes of them unto Bethel. And he put down the idolatrous priests, whom the kings of Judah had ordained to burn incense in the high places in the cities of Judah, and in the places round about Jerusalem; them also that burned incense unto Baal, to the sun, and to the moon, and to the planets, and to all the host of heaven. And he brought out the grove from the house of the LORD, without Jerusalem, unto the brook Kidron, and burned it at the brook Kidron, and stamped it small to powder, and cast the powder thereof upon the graves of the children of the people. And he brake down the houses of the sodomites, that were by the house of the LORD, where the women wove hangings for the grove. And he brought all the priests out of the cities of Judah, and defiled the high places where the priests had burned incense, from Geba to Beersheba, and brake down the high places of the gates that were in the entering in of the gate of Joshua the governor of the city, which were on a man's left hand at the gate of the city.
