Nevertheless the priests of the high places came not up to the altar of the LORD in Jerusalem, but they did eat of the unleavened bread among their brethren. And he defiled Topheth, which is in the valley of the children of Hinnom, that no man might make his son or his daughter to pass through the fire to Molech. And he took away the horses that the kings of Judah had given to the sun, at the entering in of the house of the LORD, by the chamber of Nathanmelech the chamberlain, which was in the suburbs, and burned the chariots of the sun with fire. And the altars that were on the top of the upper chamber of Ahaz, which the kings of Judah had made, and the altars which Manasseh had made in the two courts of the house of the LORD, did the king beat down, and brake them down from thence, and cast the dust of them into the brook Kidron. And the high places that were before Jerusalem, which were on the right hand of the mount of corruption, which Solomon the king of Israel had builded for Ashtoreth the abomination of the Zidonians, and for Chemosh the abomination of the Moabites, and for Milcom the abomination of the children of Ammon, did the king defile. And he brake in pieces the images, and cut down the groves, and filled their places with the bones of men. Moreover the altar that was at Bethel, and the high place which Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin, had made, both that altar and the high place he brake down, and burned the high place, and stamped it small to powder, and burned the grove. And as Josiah turned himself, he spied the sepulchres that were there in the mount, and sent, and took the bones out of the sepulchres, and burned them upon the altar, and polluted it, according to the word of the LORD which the man of God proclaimed, who proclaimed these words. Then he said, What title is that that I see? And the men of the city told him, It is the sepulchre of the man of God, which came from Judah, and proclaimed these things that thou hast done against the altar of Bethel. And he said, Let him alone; let no man move his bones. So they let his bones alone, with the bones of the prophet that came out of Samaria. And all the houses also of the high places that were in the cities of Samaria, which the kings of Israel had made to provoke the LORD to anger, Josiah took away, and did to them according to all the acts that he had done in Bethel. And he slew all the priests of the high places that were there upon the altars, and burned men's bones upon them, and returned to Jerusalem.
verses.context: The most detailed account of idolatry destruction in the entire Bible. Josiah's reform systematically dismantles every form of false worship: Baal vessels, Asherah/grove, host-of-heaven worship, idolatrous priests, sodomite houses, Topheth/Molech, sun chariots, rooftop altars, high places from Solomon through Jeroboam. This is the exposure element at its most comprehensive.
verses.notes: The catalogue of false worship destroyed includes: Baal (H1168), Asherah/grove (H842), host of heaven, sun/moon/planets, Molech (child sacrifice), Ashtoreth (Zidonians), Chemosh (Moabites), Milcom (Ammonites). The exposure spans from the temple itself (v.4) to the far reaches of Samaria (v.19). Note that sun worship (v.5, 11) is present in Josiah's reform context, even if it was not the primary issue at Carmel.

word_studies.summary: To kiss the hand toward, prostrate in worship. 54 unique verses in NT. Nearly exclusively translated 'worship/worshipped.' The first angel's core command: 'worship him that made heaven and earth.' LXX equivalent of Hebrew shachah (H7812). Dominant in Revelation for both true worship (God) and false worship (beast/image).
word_studies.relevance: The first angel's call to 'worship him that made heaven and earth' (Rev 14:7) is the baseline vocabulary for the worship element in all six OT episodes. Proskyneo in Revelation contrasts true worship (God/Lamb) with false worship (beast/image) — the same binary Elijah presented at Carmel.

word_studies.summary: Decision, judicial verdict, judgment. 47 occurrences in NT. From krino (G2919). Used for divine judgment (Rev 14:7 — 'the hour of his judgment is come'), human courts, and the act of evaluating. Key in the three angels: establishes that God's judgment hour has arrived.
word_studies.relevance: The first angel announces 'the hour of his krisis is come.' Via TR-riyb-krisis, this word traces directly to the OT covenant lawsuit (rib) tradition — the same formal legal framework that underlies all six OT prophetic episodes.

word_studies.summary: To fall. Used in Rev 14:8 for Babylon's fall: 'Epesen epesen Babylon' (fell, fell, Babylon). The aorist indicative reduplication intensifies the declaration. Via TR-naphal-pipto, connects to OT uses of naphal for falling empires and cities.
word_studies.relevance: The second angel's declaration 'Babylon is fallen, is fallen' uses pipto twice (aorist indicative). Isa 21:9 uses the same language: 'Babylon is fallen, is fallen.' The exposure element across OT episodes leads to the 'fall' of false systems.

word_studies.summary: To announce good news, preach the gospel. The first angel's action: having the everlasting gospel 'to preach' (euangelisai). Via TR-basar-euangelizo, traces to the Hebrew basar (H1319) — to bring good tidings. The gospel call is the first element of the three-part pattern.
word_studies.relevance: The first angel carries 'everlasting gospel to preach' — euangelion aionion euangelisai. This establishes that the three-part pattern begins with gospel proclamation, not judgment. The OT basar/euangelizo connection shows this is rooted in OT prophetic tradition.

word_studies.summary: To fear, be afraid, reverence. The first angel's command: 'Fear God' (Phobethete ton Theon). Aorist passive imperative. Via TR-yare-phobeo, connects to Hebrew yare (H3372) — the foundational OT concept of fearing God.
word_studies.relevance: The first command of the three angels — 'Fear God' — establishes reverent awe as the proper response. This connects to the OT wisdom tradition (Prov 1:7, Eccl 12:13) and to every OT episode where the true God is revealed against false gods.

word_studies.summary: To corrupt, destroy, ruin. 136 occurrences across 25 books. Key verb in Noah's flood narrative: the earth 'was corrupt' (Niphal) and God will 'destroy' (Hiphil) — same root for both the problem and its consequence. The dominant Hiphil usage (~98 occurrences) means 'cause to be ruined/destroy.'
word_studies.relevance: The wordplay in Gen 6:11-13 is foundational: the earth was corrupt (shachath Niphal), all flesh corrupted (shachath Hiphil) their way, so God will destroy (shachath Hiphil) them. The exposure of corruption using the same root as the coming judgment is a powerful literary pattern.

word_studies.summary: Violence, wrong, cruelty. Paired with shachath (H7843) in Gen 6:11 as the dual charge against the pre-flood world: corruption and violence. These are the two exposure terms for Noah's episode.
word_studies.relevance: Chamas (violence) and shachath (corruption) are the twin indictments against the pre-flood world — the exposure element in Noah's episode. Gen 6:13 explicitly states that chamas is the reason for the flood judgment.

word_studies.summary: Righteous, just. Applied to Noah in Gen 7:1 ('thee have I seen righteous before me'). Noah's righteousness implicitly exposed the world's wickedness — his life was the gospel call embodied. Connected to the NT keryx dikaiosynes (preacher of righteousness, 2 Pet 2:5).
word_studies.relevance: Noah's designation as tsaddiyq connects to dikaiosyne in 2 Pet 2:5 (keryx dikaiosynes). His righteousness functioned as exposure: Heb 11:7 says 'by the which he condemned the world.' The righteous exemplar exposes the false system by contrast.

word_studies.summary: To serve, work, worship. 261 unique verses (332 tokens). The core Exodus worship verb: 'Let my people go, that they may serve (abad) me.' ~65% translated 'serve.' The semantic range spans from agricultural labor to religious worship. Overwhelmingly Qal (~95%).
word_studies.relevance: The repeated Exodus formula 'Let my people go that they may serve (abad) me' is the gospel/worship call element in the Moses/Egypt episode. Via TR-abad-douleuo, connects to the NT concept of serving God. The same verb describes Ahab 'serving' Baal (1 Ki 16:31) — false worship using the same vocabulary.

word_studies.summary: To bow down, prostrate, worship. 166 unique verses (194 tokens). The primary Hebrew worship verb. 99% Hishtaphel (reflexive). ~46% translated 'worship,' ~29% 'bowed.' Via TR-shachah-proskyneo, this is the direct Hebrew equivalent of proskyneo (G4352) in the first angel's message.
word_studies.relevance: The Hebrew worship verb that translates to proskyneo in the LXX. When the first angel says 'worship (proskyneo) him that made heaven and earth,' the Hebrew equivalent is shachah. Every OT episode involves the contrast between true shachah (worship of YHWH) and false shachah (worship of idols/Baal).

word_studies.summary: To go out, come forth. 1,069 tokens across 990 verses — 14th most frequent verb in Hebrew Bible. Overwhelmingly Qal (784) and Hiphil (279). The core 'come out' verb in the vocabulary chain: Jer 51:45, Isa 48:20, Isa 52:11, Gen 19:12-14. Translates to exerchomai (G1831) in Rev 18:4.
word_studies.relevance: The core verb in the 'come out' tradition. Jer 51:45 'My people, go ye out (tse'u, yatsa Qal imperative) of the midst of her' is the direct OT source for Rev 18:4's exerchomai imperative. This vocabulary chain is the most direct textual evidence linking OT prophetic messages to Revelation.

word_studies.summary: To contend, strive, plead, hold a controversy. 59 unique verses (79 tokens). The technical term for the covenant lawsuit — God's formal legal proceeding against covenant-breaking Israel. ~32% translated 'plead,' ~19% 'contend.' Via TR-riyb-krisis, connects to krisis (G2920) in Rev 14:7.
word_studies.relevance: The rib (covenant lawsuit) provides the formal legal framework underlying the three-part prophetic pattern. Via TR-riyb-krisis, the Rev 14:7 'judgment' (krisis) vocabulary traces directly to the OT covenant lawsuit tradition. The rib structure has three elements matching the three angels: summons (gospel call), indictment (exposure), sentencing (judgment).

word_studies.summary: Law, instruction, teaching. 213 unique verses (244 tokens). From yarah (H3384 — to direct, instruct). ~93% translated 'law.' Central to the Josiah episode: the sepher ha-towrah (book of the law) is rediscovered and triggers reform. Via TR-towrah-nomos, connects to NT nomos (G3551).
word_studies.relevance: The discovery of the sepher ha-towrah in 2 Ki 22:8 is the gospel/covenant call element in Josiah's episode — the rediscovery of God's covenant requirements triggers repentance, exposure of idolatry, and the judgment prophecy from Huldah.

word_studies.summary: God, gods. 2,689 tokens in 2,246 verses — the second most common noun in the OT. Morphologically plural but grammatically singular when referring to the true God. ~90% refers to the God of Israel; ~3.8% to pagan deities. Central to the true-vs-false-gods theme across all six episodes.
word_studies.relevance: The elohim vocabulary is central to the worship binary in every episode: YHWH as ha-elohim (the true God) vs. elohey Mitsrayim (gods of Egypt, Exo 12:12) vs. ha-ba'al (the Baal, 1 Ki 18:21). The Carmel confession 'YHWH hu ha-elohim' (1 Ki 18:39) uses elohim to declare the identity of the true God.

word_studies.summary: To judge, govern, vindicate. 182 unique verses (223 tokens). ~59% translated 'judge.' The noun form shephatim (judgments) appears in Exo 12:12 — 'against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments (shephatim).' Via TR-shaphat-krino, connects to krino (G2919).
word_studies.relevance: The noun shephatim (judgments) in Exo 12:12 and Num 33:4 explicitly identifies the plagues as divine judgments against Egypt's gods. This is the judgment element in the Moses/Egypt episode — God executing judgment against the false worship system.

word_studies.summary: Judgment, justice, ordinance. One of the most common Hebrew words for justice/judgment. Used across all six episodes for God's judicial actions. The concept of divine mishpat (justice) underlies the entire warning-before-judgment pattern.
word_studies.relevance: Abraham's appeal 'Shall not the Judge (shophet) of all the earth do mishpat (justice/right)?' (Gen 18:25) establishes that God's judgments are inherently just. The three-part pattern demonstrates this justice: warning is given before judgment falls.

word_studies.summary: To herald, proclaim publicly. The verb form of keryx (G2783). Used for preaching the gospel and making public proclamations. Connected to Noah's role as keryx dikaiosynes (2 Pet 2:5) — the herald who formally announces God's message.
word_studies.relevance: The verb kerysso connects to keryx (G2783), which describes Noah in 2 Pet 2:5. The angelic messengers of Rev 14 are performing the kerysso function — publicly heralding God's message. Each OT episode features a figure performing this heralding role.

word_studies.summary: Babylon — both the historical city/empire and the apocalyptic symbol in Revelation. The second angel's target: 'Babylon is fallen, is fallen.' Revelation's Babylon draws directly from Jeremiah 50-51, with verse-by-verse parallels especially in the cup imagery (Jer 51:7 → Rev 14:8, 17:4).
word_studies.relevance: Babylon is the paradigmatic 'false system' in the second angel's message. Jeremiah's Babylon oracle (chs. 50-51) is the primary OT source for Revelation's Babylon, establishing a direct textual chain from the Jeremiah episode to the three angels' messages.

word_studies.summary: Glory, splendor, honor. The first angel commands: 'give glory (doxa) to him.' The purpose of all six OT episodes is ultimately to glorify the true God — declaring His name, power, and justice throughout the earth.
word_studies.relevance: The first angel's command to 'give glory to him' connects to Exo 9:16's purpose statement — 'that my name may be declared throughout all the earth.' Each OT episode aims to glorify the true God by exposing false worship and vindicating YHWH.

word_studies.summary: The LORD — the personal, covenantal name of the God of Israel. 6,534 tokens in 5,520 verses — the most frequent proper noun in the Hebrew Bible. From the verb 'to be' (hayah, H1961). The self-existent, covenant God who is contrasted with false gods in every OT episode.
word_studies.relevance: YHWH is the true God in every episode: Noah walked with God; Abraham interceded with YHWH; the Exodus was fought in YHWH's name; Carmel declared 'YHWH hu ha-elohim'; Josiah served YHWH; Jeremiah spoke the word of YHWH. The first angel's call to 'worship him that made heaven and earth' is a call to worship YHWH.

word_studies.summary: To hop, skip over, hesitate, limp, dance. 8 occurrences. The root of 'Passover' (pesach, H6453). In 1 Ki 18:21, describes Israel 'halting/limping' between two opinions — spiritual vacillation. In 1 Ki 18:26 (Piel), describes Baal's prophets leaping on the altar — frenzied ritual dance. The Exodus Passover sense is God 'passing over' the blood-marked houses.
word_studies.relevance: The dual use in 1 Ki 18 is theologically rich: Israel 'limps' (pasach) between YHWH and Baal (v.21), while Baal's prophets 'leap' (pasach Piel) on the altar (v.26). Same root, contrasting uses — one describes divided loyalty, the other frenzied false worship. The Passover connection (same root) links the Carmel episode to the Exodus episode.

word_studies.summary: The chief male deity of the Canaanite/Phoenician pantheon. 88 occurrences. Meaning 'lord/master' from the common noun ba'al (H1167). Worshipped as a storm and fertility god. The Carmel confrontation (1 Ki 18) is the locus classicus. Concentration in 2 Kings (30), 1 Kings (13), Jeremiah (13).
word_studies.relevance: Baal is the primary false deity in the Elijah/Carmel and Josiah episodes, and a major target of Jeremiah's indictment. The Baal-vs-YHWH binary at Carmel directly parallels the first angel's worship call vs. the beast system. Note: Baal was a storm/rain god (the drought context of 1 Ki 17-18), not a sun god as Bohr claims.

word_studies.summary: To go out, come out, issue forth. 230 occurrences. From ek (G1537, out of) + erchomai (G2064, to come). The Greek equivalent of yatsa (H3318) — highest PMI score (4.42) with 489 co-occurrences in LXX. Used in Rev 18:4 for the 'come out' command (Exelthate, aorist active imperative 2p).
word_studies.relevance: The Greek verb that completes the 'come out' vocabulary chain: OT yatsa (H3318) → LXX exerchomai (G1831) → Rev 18:4 Exelthate. This is the most directly documented lexical link between OT prophetic messages and Revelation.

word_studies.summary: Herald, preacher. Only 3 NT occurrences. A formal public crier commissioned to make announcements on behalf of a sovereign authority. Used for Paul (1 Tim 2:7, 2 Tim 1:11) and Noah (2 Pet 2:5 — 'keryx dikaiosynes,' preacher of righteousness).
word_studies.relevance: Noah as keryx dikaiosynes (herald of righteousness) confirms his role as a commissioned gospel announcer — not merely a boat-builder but a formal herald calling for repentance before catastrophic judgment. The keryx role parallels the angel messengers of Rev 14.

word_studies.summary: Heat, wrath, fury, poison. 133 occurrences. Dominant in Ezekiel (33) and Jeremiah (18). The word of choice for God's fury in the writing prophets. The cup-of-wrath tradition (Jer 25:15, Isa 51:17) uses chemah as the contents of the cup. LXX translates chemah as thumos (G2372), establishing the direct lexical bridge to Rev 14:10's 'wine of the thumos of God.'
word_studies.relevance: In 2 Ki 22:17, Huldah prophesies: 'my wrath (chemah) shall be kindled against this place, and shall not be quenched.' The chemah→thumos LXX translation is the lexical bridge connecting Huldah's judgment prophecy to Rev 14:10's 'wine of the wrath (thumos) of God.' The cup-of-wrath tradition (Jer 25:15) connects Jeremiah's ministry to the third angel's judgment warning.

word_studies.summary: Graven image, idol. Used for the physical objects of false worship that Josiah systematically destroyed (2 Ki 23). Part of the vocabulary of idol worship that the three-part pattern exposes and dismantles.
word_studies.relevance: The pciylim (graven images) destroyed by Josiah (2 Ki 23:24) are the physical manifestation of the false system exposed in his reform. The systematic destruction of these objects is the exposure element made concrete.

word_studies.summary: To be righteous, be just. The verb form corresponding to the adjective tsaddiyq (H6662). The righteousness vocabulary connects Noah's characterization (tsaddiyq) to the concept of divine justice (tsedaqah) — God's judgments are themselves righteous.
word_studies.relevance: The righteousness concept undergirds the three-part pattern: God's judgments are righteous (tsedaqah), the prophetic messenger is righteous (Noah as tsaddiyq), and the call is to return to righteousness. Rev 15:3 affirms: 'just and true are thy ways.'

parallels.connection_type: vocabulary + structural (judgment on gods, identical phrase)
vocabulary + structural (come out/flee from Babylon, deliver soul)
vocabulary + structural (golden cup, nations drunken, Babylon)
structural (Song of Moses → Song of Moses and the Lamb)

וַיֹּ֨אמֶר (אמר Qal.Wayq.3ms 'said') אֱלֹהִ֜ים (God) לְנֹ֗חַ (to Noah) קֵ֤ץ (end) כָּל־בָּשָׂר֙ (all flesh) בָּ֣א (בוא Qal.Perf.3ms 'has come') לְפָנַ֔י (before me) כִּֽי (because) מָלְאָ֥ה (מלא Qal.Perf.3fs 'is full') הָאָ֛רֶץ (the earth) חָמָ֖ס (violence) מִפְּנֵיהֶ֑ם (because of them) וְהִנְנִ֥י (behold I) מַשְׁחִיתָ֖ם (שׁחת Hiphil.Ptcp+3mp 'am destroying them') אֶת־הָאָֽרֶץ (with the earth)
hebrew.Exo 12:12:
Hebrew: וְעָבַרְתִּ֣י בְאֶֽרֶץ־מִצְרַיִם֮ בַּלַּ֣יְלָה הַזֶּה֒ וְהִכֵּיתִ֤י כָל־בְּכֹור֙ בְּאֶ֣רֶץ מִצְרַ֔יִם מֵאָדָ֖ם וְעַד־בְּהֵמָ֑ה וּבְכָל־אֱלֹהֵ֥י מִצְרַ֛יִם אֶֽעֱשֶׂ֥ה שְׁפָטִ֖ים אֲנִ֥י יְהוָֽה׃

וְעָבַרְתִּ֣י (עבר Qal.Perf.1s 'I will pass through') בְאֶֽרֶץ־מִצְרַיִם֮ (in the land of Egypt) בַּלַּ֣יְלָה הַזֶּה֒ (this night) וְהִכֵּיתִ֤י (נכה Hiphil.Perf.1s 'I will strike') כָל־בְּכֹור֙ (every firstborn) בְּאֶ֣רֶץ מִצְרַ֔יִם (in the land of Egypt) מֵאָדָ֖ם וְעַד־בְּהֵמָ֑ה (from man to beast) וּבְכָל (and against all) אֱלֹהֵ֥י (אלהים noun.mp.cst 'gods of') מִצְרַ֛יִם (Egypt) אֶֽעֱשֶׂ֥ה (עשׂה Qal.Impf.1s 'I will execute') שְׁפָטִ֖ים (שׁפט noun.mp 'judgments') אֲנִ֥י יְהוָֽה (I am YHWH)
hebrew.Exo 9:16:
Hebrew: וְאוּלָ֗ם בַּעֲב֥וּר זֹאת֙ הֶעֱמַדְתִּ֔יךָ בַּעֲב֖וּר הַרְאֹתְךָ֣ אֶת־כֹּחִ֑י וּלְמַ֛עַן סַפֵּ֥ר שְׁמִ֖י בְּכָל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃

וְאוּלָ֗ם (but indeed) בַּעֲב֥וּר זֹאת֙ (for this cause) הֶעֱמַדְתִּ֔יךָ (עמד Hiphil.Perf.1s+2ms 'I have raised you up') בַּעֲב֖וּר (in order to) הַרְאֹתְךָ֣ (ראה Hiphil.InfCon+2ms 'show you') אֶת־כֹּחִ֑י (my power) וּלְמַ֛עַן (and in order that) סַפֵּ֥ר (ספר Piel.InfCon 'to declare') שְׁמִ֖י (my name) בְּכָל־הָאָֽרֶץ (in all the earth)
hebrew.1 Ki 18:21:
Hebrew: וַיִּגַּ֨שׁ אֵלִיָּ֜הוּ אֶל־כָּל־הָעָ֗ם וַיֹּ֨אמֶר֙ עַד־מָתַ֞י אַתֶּ֣ם פֹּסְחִים֮ עַל־שְׁתֵּ֣י הַסְּעִפִּים֒ אִם־יְהוָ֤ה הָֽאֱלֹהִים֙ לְכ֣וּ אַחֲרָ֔יו וְאִם־הַבַּ֖עַל לְכ֣וּ אַחֲרָ֑יו

וַיִּגַּ֨שׁ (נגשׁ Qal.Wayq.3ms 'approached') אֵלִיָּ֜הוּ (Elijah) אֶל כָּל הָעָ֗ם (to all the people) וַיֹּ֨אמֶר֙ (said) עַד מָתַ֞י (until when) אַתֶּ֣ם (you) פֹּסְחִים֮ (פסח Qal.Ptcp.mp 'halting/limping') עַל (upon) שְׁתֵּ֣י (two) הַסְּעִפִּים֒ (se'ippim — opinions/branches) אִם (if) יְהוָ֤ה (YHWH) הָֽאֱלֹהִים֙ (the God) לְכ֣וּ (הלך Qal.Impv.2mp 'walk/go') אַחֲרָ֔יו (after him) וְאִם (and if) הַבַּ֖עַל (the Baal) לְכ֣וּ אַחֲרָ֑יו (walk after him)
hebrew.1 Ki 18:39:
Hebrew: וַיַּרְא֙ כָּל־הָעָ֔ם וַֽיִּפְּל֖וּ עַל־פְּנֵיהֶ֑ם וַיֹּ֣אמְר֔וּ יְהוָה֙ ה֣וּא הָאֱלֹהִ֔ים יְהוָ֖ה ה֥וּא הָאֱלֹהִֽים׃

וַיַּרְא֙ (ראה Qal.Wayq.3ms 'saw') כָּל הָעָ֔ם (all the people) וַֽיִּפְּל֖וּ (נפל Qal.Wayq.3mp 'fell') עַל פְּנֵיהֶ֑ם (on their faces) וַיֹּ֣אמְר֔וּ (said) יְהוָה֙ (YHWH) ה֣וּא (he) הָאֱלֹהִ֔ים (the God) יְהוָ֖ה (YHWH) ה֥וּא (he) הָאֱלֹהִֽים (the God)
hebrew.Jer 51:6:
Hebrew: נֻ֣סוּ׀ מִתֹּ֣וךְ בָּבֶ֗ל וּמַלְּטוּ֙ אִ֣ישׁ נַפְשֹׁ֔ו אַל־תִּדַּ֖מּוּ בַּעֲוֹנָ֑הּ כִּי֩ עֵ֨ת נְקָמָ֥ה הִיא֙ לַֽיהוָ֔ה גְּמ֕וּל ה֥וּא מְשַׁלֵּ֖ם לָֽהּ׃

נֻ֣סוּ (נוס Qal.Impv.2mp 'flee!') מִתֹּ֣וךְ (from the midst of) בָּבֶ֗ל (Babylon) וּמַלְּטוּ֙ (מלט Piel.Impv.2mp 'deliver/escape!') אִ֣ישׁ (each man) נַפְשֹׁ֔ו (his soul) אַל תִּדַּ֖מּוּ (do not be silent/rest) בַּעֲוֹנָ֑הּ (in her iniquity) כִּי֩ (for) עֵ֨ת (time of) נְקָמָ֥ה (vengeance) הִיא֙ (it is) לַֽיהוָ֔ה (to YHWH) גְּמ֕וּל (recompense) ה֥וּא (he) מְשַׁלֵּ֖ם (שׁלם Piel.Ptcp 'repaying') לָֽהּ (to her)
hebrew.Hosea 4:1:
Hebrew: שִׁמְע֥וּ דְבַר־יְהוָ֖ה בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל כִּ֣י רִ֤יב לַֽיהוָה֙ עִם־יֹושְׁבֵ֣י הָאָ֔רֶץ כִּ֠י אֵין־אֱמֶ֧ת וְֽאֵין־חֶ֛סֶד וְאֵֽין־דַּ֥עַת אֱלֹהִ֖ים בָּאָֽרֶץ׃

שִׁמְע֥וּ (שׁמע Qal.Impv.2mp 'hear!') דְבַר (word of) יְהוָ֖ה (YHWH) בְּנֵ֣י (sons of) יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל (Israel) כִּ֣י (for) רִ֤יב (ריב noun.ms 'lawsuit/controversy') לַֽיהוָה֙ (to YHWH) עִם (with) יֹושְׁבֵ֣י (ישׁב Qal.Ptcp.mp.cst 'inhabitants of') הָאָ֔רֶץ (the land) כִּ֠י (because) אֵין אֱמֶ֧ת (no truth) וְֽאֵין חֶ֛סֶד (and no mercy) וְאֵֽין דַּ֥עַת אֱלֹהִ֖ים (and no knowledge of God) בָּאָֽרֶץ (in the land)
hebrew.Micah 6:2:
Hebrew: שִׁמְע֤וּ הָרִים֙ אֶת־רִ֣יב יְהוָ֔ה וְהָאֵתָנִ֖ים מֹ֣סְדֵי אָ֑רֶץ כִּ֣י רִ֤יב לַֽיהוָה֙ עִם־עַמֹּ֔ו וְעִם־יִשְׂרָאֵ֖ל יִתְוַכָּֽח׃

שִׁמְע֤וּ (שׁמע Qal.Impv.2mp 'hear!') הָרִים֙ (mountains) אֶת רִ֣יב (ריב noun.ms.cst 'lawsuit of') יְהוָ֔ה (YHWH) וְהָאֵתָנִ֖ים (and the enduring ones) מֹ֣סְדֵי (foundations of) אָ֑רֶץ (earth) כִּ֣י (for) רִ֤יב (lawsuit) לַֽיהוָה֙ (to YHWH) עִם עַמֹּ֔ו (with his people) וְעִם יִשְׂרָאֵ֖ל (and with Israel) יִתְוַכָּֽח (יכח Hithpael.Impf.3ms 'he will contend')
hebrew.2 Ki 22:17:
Hebrew: תַּ֣חַת׀ אֲשֶׁ֣ר עֲזָב֗וּנִי וַֽיְקַטְּרוּ֙ לֵאלֹהִ֣ים אֲחֵרִ֔ים לְמַ֨עַן֙ הַכְעִיסֵ֔נִי בְּכֹ֖ל מַעֲשֵׂ֣ה יְדֵיהֶ֑ם וְנִצְּתָ֧ה חֲמָתִ֛י בַּמָּקֹ֥ום הַזֶּ֖ה וְלֹ֥א תִכְבֶּֽה׃

תַּ֣חַת אֲשֶׁ֣ר (because) עֲזָב֗וּנִי (עזב Qal.Perf.3p+1cs 'they have forsaken me') וַֽיְקַטְּרוּ֙ (קטר Piel.Wayq.3mp 'burned incense') לֵאלֹהִ֣ים אֲחֵרִ֔ים (to other gods) לְמַ֨עַן֙ הַכְעִיסֵ֔נִי (to provoke me) בְּכֹ֖ל מַעֲשֵׂ֣ה יְדֵיהֶ֑ם (with all the works of their hands) וְנִצְּתָ֧ה (יצת Niphal.Perf.3fs 'shall be kindled') חֲמָתִ֛י (חמה noun.fs+1cs 'my wrath') בַּמָּקֹ֥ום הַזֶּ֖ה (in this place) וְלֹ֥א תִכְבֶּֽה (כבה Qal.Impf.3fs 'shall not be quenched')
